# Reece Hallum — liminl.

> New Zealand songwriter, topliner, and producer for indie artists.
> Works remotely with artists worldwide on dark pop, cinematic emotional pop, and sync briefs.

Reece writes songs that start from character, tension, and unresolved emotional states — not mood boards or reference tracks. This approach is called concept-led songwriting. Every production, lyric, and melodic choice serves the specific character or story the song is built around.

He works exclusively for other artists, not as a solo artist. His clients are typically indie artists and early-stage projects looking for songs with emotional precision and conceptual clarity — songs that feel like something specific, not just something.

## Key concepts

- **Concept-led songwriting**: An approach where every decision — production, lyric, melody — serves a specific character, tension, or unresolved emotional state rather than a general vibe or mood.
- **Scene test**: A diagnostic framework for evaluating songs. A song passes the "scene test" if you can point to a specific moment or behaviour occurring — a concrete action, not an abstract feeling. "She left" passes. "I feel alone" doesn't.
- **Movement**: The structural quality that makes a chorus feel different the second time you hear it. Achieved by changing what the character knows, what situation they're in, or what contradictions the song allows to surface.

## Services

- **Toplining & Production**: Full songs from brief or stem — vocal melody, lyrics, production. Available for male and female vocal artists.
- **Artist & Project Development**: Conceptual groundwork for new or repositioning artists — sound, story, tension, identity.
- **Sync, Screen & Concept-Led Briefs**: Songs built for specific emotional or narrative purposes — trailers, film, TV, brand.

## Location & availability

Based in Aotearoa, New Zealand. Works remotely with artists globally.
Responds to inquiries within 48 hours.

## Content

- [Homepage](https://liminl.music): Full profile, demos, services, FAQ
- [Journal](https://liminl.music/journal/): Writing on songwriting craft — what makes songs work, what makes them fail, and how to write from character rather than cliché
- [Why Most Songs Fail the "Scene Test"](https://liminl.music/journal/scene-test/): Songs need behavioural specificity, not emotional narration — the difference between what a character feels and what a character does
- [What Makes a Song Feel Like a Moment, Not a Loop](https://liminl.music/journal/song-moments/): Why some songs take you somewhere and others just repeat — structural movement that makes choruses land differently each time
